question.text: In the SEBI vs Marfatia Stock Broking case, what was the outcome regarding the allegations?

answer.text: The Adjudicating Officer did not impose any monetary penalty as the observations were procedural in nature and were complied with after being pointed out
A penalty of Rs. 5 lakh was imposed
A penalty of Rs. 2 lakh was imposed under Section 15HB

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** In the Marfatia case, the AO found that: (1) although a separate AML policy was not documented initially, requirements were incorporated in KYC and RMS policies, (2) the Principal Officer was appointed on time (February 2006, shortly after the January 2006 circular), and (3) observations were procedural and corrected after inspection. No monetary penalty was imposed.
**The Trap:** This is the only case in the chapter where NO penalty was imposed. It demonstrates that if violations are procedural and promptly corrected, leniency is possible.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Compare Marfatia (zero penalty for prompt compliance) with Shreepati (Rs. 3 lakh for 2-year delay in corrections). The lesson across NISM exams is: fix compliance gaps immediately when identified.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The Marfatia case is your best-case scenario. If an inspection finds deficiencies, correct them IMMEDIATELY. Document the corrections and timelines. Prompt remediation can be the difference between no penalty and a significant fine.
